item.description:
Everyone says they're building an AI-native company. But what does that mean?

Automating what you're already doing with AI is not enough. A truly AI-native company rethinks every role and tears down the walls between them.

- QA doesn't just file bugs: they point agents at them and file the fixes too
- PMs don't just ship a 70%-done PRD and wait for eng to hit the edge cases: they point agents to walk the real code, surface those cases, and flush them out with test cases
- - Designers don't just land up with Figma mocks: they prototype on real Android 12 devices 😜 to test how those animations and UX actually hold up
- Data scientists don't just wait for real data: they run simulations up front, generate synthetic data, and build intuition around which A/B option wins

So what's left for engineers? Plenty.

I wrote a sketch of what that might look like, in detail, here: https://kau.sh/blog/ai-native-company/

in an effort to prove that i'm not just an AI tip dispensing bot, here's a palate cleanser of one my (& the 90s') all time favorite songs, from a podcast that does some of the best song breakdowns:

https://songexploder.net/goo-goo-dolls

if you work at a slightly larger company and you're not already playing with open models, building a sense for when to reach for which, you're going to have a lot of catching up to do soon...

3-4 times now I've liked GLM 5.2's answer more than 5.5 xhigh or Opus 4.8.

bonkers! even if the bar were just 80% as good as the leading models, the cost alone makes open models an easy call, especially as they build out their loops. approaching better results?

the tide, 'tis a-shifting, friends...

I spent sometime on loop engineering. Some notes...

Two types of loops: 1️⃣ one-shot loops (solved) and 2️⃣ autonomous

I (personally) don't think "let the agent merge" is the right bar yet. Loops are better at producing reviewable stuff you still sign off on.

Every loop I've built so far ended up being a little bespoke. It's hard to find a single template for all the types of loops.

I think that's actually good. It's probably going to be the work software engineers have to actually spend time "engineering".

Loops need bounds (a loop with no max turns is ~= a while(true) in prod) and agents need to be told "do not absorb uncertainty."

To make this all financially feasible - tier your models. Cheap local for the always-on stuff, frontier only when it counts.

Lastly, a new practice that's been really working well for me is "HTML workpads" to do all my review. Each task maintains context/logs/progress etc. in a visual HTML.

More in my post: https://kau.sh/blog/responsible-loop-engineering/

I don't want to paint myself into any single major AI lab corner yet, so I've been kicking the tires on open source agent harnesses.

OpenCode has become my daily driver. I took some time to write up intermediate+ user tips that show where it really shines.

Really hoping open source harnesses get more traction and push the competition forward. I know I'm super happy with OpenCode + OpenChamber right now, and it'll be hard to move away from it at this point.

Full Post - https://kau.sh/blog/opencode-power-user-tips/

maybe it’s the optimist in me but i feel there’s a reckoning coming where engineers are going to be evaluated by how effectively they use AI and it’s an actual trait companies start to look for in interviews.

tokensculpting = !tokenmaxxing

prompt engineering → context engineering → harness engineering

but harness engineering finally feels like "real engineering". no more woo woo stuff as @iurysouza.dev says 😅.

Episode 307 is one of our most useful ones, on how to think about AI-assisted engineering.

Listen to the full episode!

Youtube https://youtu.be/fmbbelimwfk?si=vsiwg3BupGUxJTWq&t=893

Spotify https://open.spotify.com/episode/150VMJDqUfLQ9GdhPjZfOO?si=O1Khk4AmQKiCVQ013NxufQ

https://fragmentedpodcast.com/episodes/307/?t=14:53
/btw is one of the most under-rated claude code commands, that other harnesses should straight up steal

when generating code is cheap, the spec starts to feel more valuable.

i keep thinking about my own tools: usbi went from shell → Go → Rust → .kts. Podsync went from Python → Rust.

implementation changed rapidly but spec stayed the same

feels like the shape of a lot more software...

Agents are the new compilers. Specs are the new code.

https://kau.sh/blog/agent-compilers/

Love how Mitchell said of the current AI hypsters: If there's all this great value being created with AI... where are the actual products?

For the full episode:

Youtube: https://youtu.be/pKrsF3eQQnQ?si=4flNBqaVwFer8UkF
Website: https://fragmentedpodcast.com/episodes/310
Spotify: https://open.spotify.com/episode/0zhtEuDpvxyi2hnlcEj522?si=lO6FWFk3SSCx15QtlNpGmw

RE: https://mas.to/@fragmented/116401629824250206

I look up to @mitchellh as one of the best developer voices today.this ep. nearly broke me to edit-every 10s was a 💡 moment and I kept going "nope, can't cut that either."

Can't thank him enough for being so gracious with his time.proud @fragmented moment for me & @iurysza

RE: https://mas.to/@fragmented/116333355868122694

So you’re an android dev and got 4 worktrees going, with gradle daemons roaring and frying your machine … wdyd?

you go to the ☁️ with background agents ! listen to the newest episode to find out more.

Lots of "I built this with AI" posts lately. Here's one where I actually enjoyed the building part.

Introducing PodSync (for editors): a podcast alignment tool (uses VAD, MFCC fingerprinting, x-correlation).

AI helped me 👨‍💻 it in Rust but yrs of aligning tracks 👂helped with the algo!

Wrote up the full approach with an interactive demo:

- https://kau.sh/blog/podsync
- https://github.com/kaushikgopal/podsync

i like a lot of the new tmux-ish emulators like cmux but the smoothest workflow i've found is just using ghostty & one main tmux session.

☝️ one window for each task i'm working on with name "i" recognize quickly.

then use fzf to search the tmux window list pane & switch ⚡

RE: https://mas.to/@fragmented/116126821033957142

This was fun for both of us!

We share some of how the fragmented 🌭 is made + Tips from @iurysza and me, and a crazy way we used AI to solve very real audio problems 😉.

opencode is basically openclaw for coding.

an interesting focus area in the world of agent orchestration is "access fluidity".

how easy is it to reach the agents doing the work on your machine - from your phone, terminal, IDE, or browser?

it's tempting to think of it as just an open-source Claude Code/Codex variant.

it's def. more ! built on a server-client architecture, it makes hopping between mobile, desktop, and web super easy.

https://kau.sh/blog/opencode-openclaw/

RE: https://mas.to/@fragmented/116084718264714391

new episode is out! spot the easter 🥚?

recent models have shown massive improvement owing to clever use of "modes" and subagent dispatch. it really clicked for me after chatting with @iurysza

listen to improve your fundamentals! (not just tactics)
I used to love Keynote for my slide decks but I've since become a full @iapresenter convert (from the folks @ia ). tastefully chosen constraints and display, all with the power of markdown.

RE: https://mas.to/@fragmented/116039415103907028

agent skills are the most powerful construct for AI coding today. it's the quickest way to get better results with less context bloat.

but with great power comes responsibility... they can become a prompt injection vector.

this episode is a crash course on agent skills! 🎧

PSA: the official Anthropic GitHub org is github.com/anthropics (plural "s")

not the lucky but slightly disturbing zombie shooter github.com/anthropic

if you're installing official Claude plugins or skills and 404ing... now you know. 's' doing some heavy lifting there.
